title: Starting a Jewish Study Group in Your City
author: Torah Light Editorial Team
target_audience: People wanting to start Torah study

historical_periods_covered.chinese_equivalent: 春秋战国时期 (Spring and Autumn / Warring States Period)
historical_periods_covered.relevance: Origins of communal Torah study and houses of learning
Joshua ben Perachiah's teaching on acquiring study partners
Kaifeng Jewish community maintained study groups despite isolation
Jewish study circles continued alongside Confucian academies

english.title: Starting a Jewish Study Group in Your City

chinese_simplified.title: 在你的城市建立犹太学习小组

sources: Women of Reform Judaism: Book Club Guide
header_image_prompt: A warm, inviting Chinese brushstroke-style scene showing a small group of diverse people gathered around a low wooden table in a traditional Chinese-style courtyard. On the table are open Jewish texts with Hebrew letters, tea cups, and dim lighting from paper lanterns. Through an archway, a traditional Chinese garden is visible with bamboo and a reflecting pool. The atmosphere is intimate and studious, with gentle warm ochre and deep ink tones. Figures are rendered in minimalist brushstrokes suggesting concentration and connection. Style: traditional shan shui ink wash meets contemporary community life.
practical_features: Step-by-step guide to starting a study group
Discussion techniques (Hevruta, Round Table, Controversial Statement)
Tips for handling diverse knowledge levels
Cultural sensitivity guidance for Asian/Chinese contexts
cultural_bridges: Comparison of Jewish and Chinese scholarly traditions
Confucian academies (shuyuan) parallel to batei midrash
